chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: All right, let's get started. Welcome back to 006. Today we are doing some of the coolest

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: data structures we will see in this class, maybe some of the coolest data structures

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: ever. Binary trees. You've certainly seen trees in many forms in the past, including

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: in this class we've talked about trees as a lower bound tool in the decision tree model.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: But this lecture and the next lecture we're going to build one data structure that is

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: almost superior to all data structures we have seen and can do almost anything really

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: fast. First, recall all the data structures we've seen so far. Arrays, linked lists, dynamic

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: arrays, sorted arrays, hash tables. And the two sets of operations we're interested in

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: supporting. The two interfaces. One was sequences, where we're maintaining items in a specified

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: order. We want to be able to insert an item right after another item or delete an item

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: in the middle of the list and always be able to access the ith item. We haven't seen any

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: good data structures for that problem. We're really good at inserting and deleting at the

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: beginning or the end of the sequence. But we haven't seen anything that's efficient

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: at inserting in the middle of the list or deleting in the middle of the list. Linked

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: list, you can't even get to the middle in less than linear time. Array, you can get

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: to the middle, but if you make any changes, you have to do the shift, which is very expensive.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: So today, or sorry, next lecture, for the first time, we will see all of those operations

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: efficient. I'll mention our goal. Where efficient means logarithmic. So we're not quite as good

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: as linked lists and dynamic arrays at inserting and deleting at the ends. Those there that

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: we achieve constant or constant amortized time. But up to this log factor, we're going

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: to get the best of all worlds, where we can solve all the things, all the operations that

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: don't build or iterate through the entire structure. That, of course, takes linear time.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: But we can do all the others in logarithmic time. For sets, sets, we're maintaining a

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: bunch of items which have intrinsic keys, and we want to search by key. So hash tables

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: are great if you're only doing exact searches. If you want to find a key and get yes or no,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: is it in there? And if it's in there, give me the item. That's what Python dictionaries

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: do. And they're great at inserting and deleting. But they're really bad at find previous and

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: find next. This is the unsuccessful case. If I search for a key and it's not in my structure,

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: I would like to know more than just the answer no. I'd like to know what the previous and

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: next items that are actually in the structure. So what are my nearest matches when I search

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: by key? That's a natural query. And the only data structure we have that's good at it is

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: a sorted array, because binary search gives this to us. If we search for a key by binary

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: search and we don't find it, the position that we end up at is right between the previous

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: and next one. But, of course, sorted arrays are terrible for dynamic operations. We don't

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: know how to maintain. We can't maintain a sorted array without any gaps when we're doing

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: insertions and deletions. In some sense, today and next class, binary trees let us represent

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: a sorted order or, in general, an order of items dynamically and still allow us to do

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: very fast things like get at of i and find previous of the key. So that's our goal. We're

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: not going to quite get to this goal today. We're going to get an incomparable thing called

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: the height of the tree. And then on Thursday, we'll be able to finish and achieve this goal.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: Today is just in service to that goal.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: So what is a binary tree? Let me draw an example and then define it more precisely. Mathematicians

chunks.text: picture. So this is an example of a binary tree. It has a bunch of nodes, which we're

chunks.title: Lec

chunks.text: Maybe an item, or there's a node, it had an item in it, and it had a next pointer, and

chunks.text: it had a previous pointer. So in some sense, if it's doubly linked, we had a previous pointer.

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: of linked lists, especially singly linked lists, if you just have one pointer per node,

chunks.text: you can only build a list. And so the result is this node is going to have depth linear.

chunks.text: of what's going on. Now today, we're going to talk about the height of a tree. So let

chunks.title: Lec
chunks.text: the depth of a node. Depth of a node is, I guess, the number of its ancestors. That's

text: All right, let's get started. Welcome back to 006. Today we are doing some of the coolest data structures we will see in this class, maybe some of the coolest data structures ever. Binary trees. You've certainly seen trees in many forms in the past, including in this class we've talked about trees as a lower bound tool in the decision tree model. But this lecture and the next lecture we're going to build one data structure that is almost superior to all data structures we have seen and can do almost anything really fast. First, recall all the data structures we've seen so far. Arrays, linked lists, dynamic arrays, sorted arrays, hash tables. And the two sets of operations we're interested in supporting. The two interfaces. One was sequences, where we're maintaining items in a specified order. We want to be able to insert an item right after another item or delete an item in the middle of the list and always be able to access the ith item. We haven't seen any good data structures for that problem. We're really good at inserting and deleting at the beginning or the end of the sequence. But we haven't seen anything that's efficient at inserting in the middle of the list or deleting in the middle of the list. Linked list, you can't even get to the middle in less than linear time. Array, you can get to the middle, but if you make any changes, you have to do the shift, which is very expensive. So today, or sorry, next lecture, for the first time, we will see all of those operations efficient. I'll mention our goal. Where efficient means logarithmic. So we're not quite as good as linked lists and dynamic arrays at inserting and deleting at the ends. Those there that we achieve constant or constant amortized time.
